abstract: Unveiling the nature of active sites under realistic reaction conditions is a fundamental challenge in the development of efficient single-atom catalysts (SACs). In this study, we utilize a model catalyst consisting of iron phthalocyanine immobilized on carbon nanotubes (FePc-CNT) to investigate the structural and electronic dynamics of atomically dispersed iron centers during the electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR). By integrating operando $^{57}$Fe Mössbauer, X-ray absorption (XAS), and Raman spectroscopies, we track the evolution of the iron sites from their initial high-spin (HS) $O_2$-Fe(III)N$_4$ resting state. Our findings demonstrate that under cathodic potential, the iron center undergoes a reduction to a HS Fe(II)N$_4$ state. Crucially, we observe distinct behaviors depending on the electrolyte environment: while the centers are prone to irreversible reduction to metallic iron under Ar, the presence of CO$_2$ stabilizes the site through the formation of a HS Fe(II)Pc$^–$-CO$_2^–$ intermediate. This species involves a one-electron reduction of the phthalocyanine ligand rather than the metal center itself. Theoretical calculations support these experimental observations, revealing that the electroreduction of the Pc ligand shifts the d-band center of the iron, optimizing the binding strength of CO$_2$ and significantly enhancing catalytic turnover. This work provides a comprehensive atomic-level picture of the synergistic role between metal centers and organic ligands in SACs, offering critical insights for the rational design of CO2RR electrocatalysts.
